result:
:::writing
There’s a particular kind of quiet that only shows up after something ends. Not the quiet of an empty room, but the kind that lingers in the air after a door closes, as if the walls themselves are listening for footsteps that won’t come back. It’s a strange quiet—full, almost crowded—with everything that used to be there.

I think people underestimate how loud absence can be.

We imagine loss as a clean subtraction, like erasing chalk from a board. But it’s not like that. It’s more like writing over something again and again until the page becomes thick and indented, the original words still pressing faintly through. You can run your fingers over it and feel what used to be written, even if you can’t quite read it anymore.

Time doesn’t really fix that. It just teaches you how to stop tracing the grooves so often.

There’s a bench in a park I used to pass every morning. Nothing special about it—green paint peeling, one leg slightly shorter than the others so it wobbles if you sit on the far left. For a long time, an older man would sit there feeding birds. He had this routine: a paper bag, always folded the same way, always set beside him before he opened it. The birds knew him. They’d gather before he even arrived, like they had some invisible clock tuned to his steps.

Then one day he just… wasn’t there.

At first, it didn’t feel like anything. People miss days. Routines slip. But the birds still came. They waited. They lingered longer than usual, hopping closer to the bench, pecking at nothing. Watching.

Days passed. Then weeks.

Eventually, fewer birds showed up. Not all at once—just a gradual thinning, like a crowd slowly realizing the show isn’t happening. The bench stayed the same. The park stayed the same. Morning light still filtered through the trees in that soft, forgiving way. But something had shifted, and you could feel it if you paid attention.

That’s the thing about patterns. When they break, they don’t shatter loudly. They unravel quietly.

I sometimes wonder how many invisible routines we’re all part of in other people’s lives. The person who notices when you cross the street at the same time every day. The barista who starts making your drink when you walk in. The neighbor who hears your footsteps through the ceiling and subconsciously measures their evening by the rhythm of yours.

We like to think we move through the world independently, but we’re constantly leaving traces in each other’s days. Small, almost imperceptible imprints. And when one of those imprints disappears, something subtle goes off-balance.

Not enough to make headlines. Not enough to stop anything. Just enough to be felt.

There’s a word I once tried to find for that feeling—the quiet awareness of a missing presence that wasn’t central to your life, but somehow still mattered. I never found one that fit exactly. Language is good at naming big things: love, grief, joy, anger. But it struggles with the in-between spaces, the almosts, the nearlys.

Maybe that’s why we tell stories.

Stories let us hold onto those in-between things a little longer. They stretch moments that would otherwise slip past unnoticed. They give shape to feelings that don’t quite have names.

Like the way a room feels different after laughter has been in it. Or the way certain songs seem to carry entire versions of yourself inside them—people you used to be, places you used to go, thoughts you used to think without realizing they would someday become memories.

It’s strange to think about how many versions of ourselves we leave behind as we move forward. Not in a dramatic, shedding-skin kind of way. More like a trail of faint echoes.

There’s the version of you who believed something wholeheartedly that you no longer believe. The version who loved someone you don’t talk to anymore. The version who thought they had everything figured out, only to realize later how much they didn’t know.

Those versions don’t disappear. They just become quieter.

Sometimes they show up unexpectedly. In a reaction you didn’t expect to have. In a place that feels oddly familiar. In the way you hesitate before saying something, even if you’re not sure why.

It’s tempting to think of growth as a straight line—a steady progression from one point to another. But it’s not. It’s more like a loop with a forward drift. You revisit things, but from slightly different angles each time.

That’s how change really works. Not as a clean break, but as a gradual reorientation.

The bench in the park is still there. Someone else sits on it now, sometimes. Not regularly. No routine has formed around it. The birds don’t gather the same way. They pass through, briefly, as if remembering something they can’t quite place.

I still walk past it.

At first, I kept expecting the man to come back. Not in a hopeful way, exactly—just a quiet assumption that the pattern would resume. That whatever pause had occurred would resolve itself.

But patterns don’t owe us continuity.

That realization is both unsettling and, in a strange way, freeing.

Because if things can end without ceremony, they can also begin without it.

Not every meaningful moment announces itself. Not every important shift arrives with clarity or intention. Sometimes, something small just… starts. A new route. A new habit. A new conversation that slowly becomes a familiar one.

And before you know it, you’ve become part of someone else’s invisible routine.

There’s a comfort in that, even if it’s fragile.

We are, all of us, passing through each other’s lives in ways we rarely fully understand. Leaving impressions we may never see. Being shaped by people who may never know the extent of their impact.

The quiet after something ends makes space for something else to begin. Not as a replacement—nothing really replaces anything—but as a continuation of movement.

The bench is still just a bench.

But it has held a hundred small histories. It has been a constant in a series of changing patterns. It has witnessed presence, absence, waiting, forgetting.

Maybe that’s all any place—or any person—really does.

Hold space.

Not forever. Not perfectly. Just long enough for something to happen.

And then, eventually, long enough for something to stop.

That quiet that follows? It’s not empty.

It’s full of everything that passed through.
:::writing

:::writing
